findings.title: Encrypted setup container blocks pre-deployment security review
findings.description: The provided setup artifact is an OpenSSL password-encrypted blob. In the current delivered form, the installer contents, package type, setup logic, and any embedded scripts cannot be inspected before decryption.
findings.evidence: Observed file type: 'openssl enc'd data with salted password'; 'Salted__' header present; no plaintext package signature or installer contents visible before decryption.
findings.impact: Security review is blocked at the exact point where trust decisions would normally happen. Malicious, outdated, or unsafe install logic could remain hidden until decryption and execution, increasing supply-chain and deployment risk.
findings.remediation: Require a controlled pre-deployment review flow: obtain the correct key material, decrypt into a separate analysis directory, identify the inner package type, and review scripts/configuration before any installation or execution.
findings.hardening: Treat encrypted installer artifacts as containers only, not trusted installers. Pair them with a review workflow that verifies contents before deployment on Raspberry Pi targets.
references: file output: openssl enc'd data with salted password

findings.title: No companion trust metadata observed with the encrypted installer artifact
findings.description: In the current evidence set, the encrypted blob was delivered without an accompanying manifest, detached signature, or separate integrity metadata file in the same artifact location.
findings.evidence: Current artifact set under engagements/playerv2-artifacts contains the encrypted file and quick-scan notes only; no detached signature or manifest was observed alongside the provided blob during enumeration.
findings.impact: Operators cannot independently verify what the encrypted blob is supposed to contain or whether the artifact matches an expected release, increasing tamper-resistance and provenance blind spots.
findings.remediation: Distribute encrypted setup blobs with a detached signature, signed manifest, or externally verified checksum package so provenance and integrity can be validated before decryption or deployment.
findings.hardening: Separate confidentiality from trust. Encryption hides contents, but integrity and release provenance should still be verifiable before install-time decisions are made.
references: enum summary: no companion manifest/checksum/signature observed

findings.title: Password-managed installer packaging creates key-handling and recovery risk
findings.description: The artifact uses a password-based OpenSSL container format. This packaging model creates an operational dependency on correct passphrase handling before the installer can be reviewed or recovered for troubleshooting.
findings.evidence: OpenSSL salted password-based format confirmed by file signature and header analysis; artifact contents remain inaccessible without the correct decryption material.
findings.impact: Poor passphrase handling can delay incident response, block installer review, encourage unsafe sharing of secrets, or push operators toward bypass workflows that weaken security and auditability.
findings.remediation: Document secure key custody, recovery, and handoff procedures for encrypted installer artifacts. Ensure authorized reviewers can decrypt copies in a controlled analysis environment without modifying the original blob.
findings.hardening: Use an installation release process that defines who holds decryption material, how it is rotated, and how decrypted review copies are controlled and disposed of.
references: quick triage blocked from inner-content review until decryption

enhancements.recommendation: Require every encrypted installer to go through a decrypt-then-review workflow in an isolated analysis directory before deployment on production or test Raspberry Pi devices.
Publish detached signatures or signed manifests alongside encrypted setup blobs so confidentiality and integrity are handled independently.
Define who holds setup decryption material, how it is recovered, and how authorized reviewers access it without normalizing password sharing through chat or ad-hoc notes.
